text:
There's a common trope that goes something like: people who have become cool and unique in some way had some version of a f*cked up childhood.

And thinking in terms of human motivation, what it is that makes us move and struggle, I can't disagree with this, and it kinda distresses me.

Does a good childhood make you uninteresting? Is utopia a world of uninteresting, barely conscious people?

To me, subjectively, it feels like this is maybe true. But I know a few very interesting people who also seem to be relatively undamaged, and that gives me hope.

I want it to be possible for there to be a world of highly conscious people who aren't f*cked up.

text:
I never grew up with dogs, always a little awkward around them, but recently had to spend a night taking care of a puppy my friend found in the woods. Just me and the little dude for a night.

And from the experience I suspect that just as much as we created the dog, we also co-evolved with the dog.

I really felt like I was tapping into some primal instincts.

With zero prior experience, I found it effortless to communicate with this tiny creature, to know what he needed when he needed it, to convey to him what I wanted him to do. When we made eye contact I felt an instant connection, an instant trust.

And then when he left there was a powerful imprint: still looking out for him when I would open the fridge, still seeing spontaneous images of him in my head, stuff like that. Usually takes a lot longer than a night for me to habituate like that.

idk but I suspect "dog" has made it into our firmware and I wonder if dog people just take that for granted, having grown up with them.

text:
I used to do this and thinking about this regarding myself it's actually a complicated question with multiple layers. In no particular order:

Personality. I'm an aggressive type who wants to change the people around me into copies of myself. This has nothing to do with atheism, I was like this before I was an atheist, I was like this while I was an atheist, and I'm like this post-atheism (I like to think I'm a bit more mindful of this tendency in myself now tho. A bit.)

Validation. I was raised a believer and still feared I might be wrong. I debated most aggressively specifically about the aspects I was most afraid of. If I could "win" the debate, it would provide added certainty that I was correct.

Crusade. I took it upon myself to liberate all the poor saps still trapped in superstition.

Externalized self-liberation. Throwing off religion did not cure me of my social hangups or other neuroses. It was supposed to but it didn't. It must be because I'm surrounded by an environment that is still stifled and therefore stifling. If I convert my environment, I will no longer pick up the oppressive energy, and then I will be actually psychologically free. (None of this was really conscious, I am trying to describe the underlying psychodynamics as best as I can remember the flow of feelings at the time. It was also obviously very confused.)

Simple comfort. It doesn't feel good to be exposed to opinions odious to me. They must be rooted out, then they will stop bothering me (btw this is pretty much how our politics works imo).

The above are of course interrelated with each other.

In my atheist days I spent time in both places.

text:
You maybe already can but it'd be cool if you could ask a court (if for a fee) to simulate a certain type of lawsuit and see what gets decided, and have that decision be valid precedent.

I'm thinking of Microsoft declaring that Windows 10 will no longer be receiving security updates, and then putting out an update today. If they keep doing updates, then actually stop somewhere down the line, does their history of doing it after the warning constitute creating an expectation and open them up to liability when somebody gets pwned, even in spite of the warning?

When suppose really they are doing the continued security updates as an act of goodwill but do not want to be bound to it anymore?

I can imagine Microsoft going to a court and saying "hey what would you decide if somebody brought such-and-such a lawsuit?" and trying to get a precedent established beforehand.

(this specifically situation is probably totally fine, I don't know anything about law aside from dumb movies, just interested in this as a more general hypothetical)

text:
Today I sat on a park bench and read this sign explaining in gory detail what feeding the wildlife does to the environment, how illegal it is and what the penalties are, and how to report it.

Then some couple and their young kid walked up right next to the sign and started tossing breakfast cereal to the birds from a big bag. Kid seemed to be having fun and ofc the ducks were eating it up.

I chose not to narc, nor even to politely point out the sign, and I still can't decide if that was cowardice or decency.

text:
Suppose this is true, and further, suppose a deeper reason for the average person being upset amounts to not wanting 47 to get a win.

For one group of people, the front-and-center experience of their homeland oppressed and liberated. For another, the indignation of watching the Most Repulsive collect a W.

Something more abstract and removed. Something more on the level of team sports.

Just entirely orthogonal motivational structures, on the deep level.

The disturbing implication of this, coupled with the sheer power of the USA, is the degree to which the fate of everybody on planet Earth is bound up in the aggregate psychological neuroses taking place in our little corner of it.

text:
"Master of your domain" would never have happened had the FCC allowed Seinfeld's writers to directly refer to masturbation.

"Uncle fucker" was going to be "motherfucker" but the MPAA threatened to rate the South Park movie NC-17 over it, so it was changed and became something much more memorable.

Obscenity is not the only thing affected by this principle. The physical constraints of what you can make appear on a frame of film, and the effort and engineering that went into in overcoming these constraints, is responsible for the great pre-CGI movies: the choices of plots and settings and characters and themes were naturally all made with these constraints in back of mind.

Constraint is part of the creative process and the total freedom from constraint brought by e.g. CGI is an obstacle in itself.

text:
Sometimes I am in particular headspace I struggle to describe. I feel whole, singleminded, ambitious, optimistic, perhaps a bit prone to delivering lighthearted mockery. This is irrespective of the situation: I don't have to be bro'ing out on some project, I can be alone and getting ready to go to bed and be in this exact headspace.

Whenever I hit it I'm always like "aha, this is how I'm supposed to live"

Wish I could put it in a bottle. Is it a certain configuration of hormones? Which SF bro is working on a thing where I can do a little blood draw as a snapshot and then reproduce the drawn configuration of hormones at will?

text:
When you start living rightly, so many things start to become different and you can be so blown away by any one of these differences that you mistake it for the essence of the thing.

So for example, "feeling your feelings" is a thing that's naturally going to happen when you start taking responsibility for your life. The old excuses die away, the old escapist substances are being avoided, and suddenly you're face-to-face with all this gunk, and you have to figure out how to deal with it so you can get on with things. And maybe you don't know right away how to deal with it. So it's just there, and you're feeling it, and then the magic happens. A bunch of breakthroughs happen because you never did that before, and it's all so novel and sublime.

At this point you're so oneshotted that you think, "Feeling my feelings was the whole thing all along!" You write a book on how everybody just needs to Feel Their Feelings, going into exquisite detail about how profound it will be. But then people read it and try the exercises and it's all kind of meh for them, they don't get what the big deal is, because in fact the book is missing the point. The point was always to take responsibility for your life. That's the actual thing that needed to be done. That you end up feeling your feelings (and any number of other things that living rightly causes, that you might end up blown away by) is in fact a side effect.

text:
I really do seem to have completely nuked my identity.

And with it, my entire motivational structure, which I painstakingly erected over the course of my whole life and whose purpose was to get me to live up to that identity.

I literally have no idea what I want to do with myself besides eat and sleep and occasionally make some money so I can continue to do the above. Libido is zero. I'll go on drives and hikes out of some vague sense that it's better than sitting around. But the valence difference between that and sitting around all day at home is in fact zero.

I don't have any relationships I care about. They were all part of the old identity and they all feel fake now and I mostly try to avoid them.

At least I'm managing to maintain a hard rule against substances. Otherwise I'd probably just d***k myself to d***h. That's been the only thing interesting enough to even slightly awaken any real eros.

I have achieved a few real and interesting things in my life, things that are living on, with my involvement behind me. I do sometimes like to check up on them and see how they're faring. Like some kind of grandpa.

I guess you could call this depression but it doesn't have any of that acute "omg heeelp I'm sooooo depressed" feeling of past times I've been depressed. Come to think of it I don't know if this or that is what the word "depression" is supposed to refer to.

And I think I did all this on purpose. Not realizing what it would actually mean to take my spiritual obsessions seriously. This stuff wasn't performative with me. Well this appears to be the result.

I expect I'll come out of this ok. Some writings suggest this is a normal part of the process.

Just in a weird place right now.

The big ethical standpoints all lay claim to each other:

Deontology:
Utilitarianism - Part of fulfilling your duties is being effective at them, so you will have to examine outcomes
Virtue ethics - You're not gonna be able to fulfill your duties if your habits are ass, get them in line

Virtue ethics:
Utilitarianism - "Road to hell is paved with good intentions", part of good character is seeing things through and adjusting
Deontology: It's your duty to be a virtue ethicist lol

Utilitarianism:
Virtue ethics: Evaluation of consequences is best done with a sound mind, execution is best done with a sound mind and body, thus you must cultivate character
Deontology: It's your duty to be a utilitarian lol

text:
One nice thing about having exploded my life again and moved to a new city is I get to set better boundaries with prospective friends.

I have political opinions, I like to think without too much ego investment, but I have them and I vote. And in my early adulthood I was so eager for acceptance that I allowed so-called friends to make pretending to agree with their politics a condition of them thinking I was a worthy person.

My self-abasement on this score got so bad I even continued my association with a person after they posted on Facebook that if you didn't vote for so-and-so they wanted you out of their life. I simply lied and continued the association. I lied and held my tongue for YEARS.

Going forward I will no longer be accepting this behavior from people who would like me to be in their life. If somebody makes an ultimatum like that I will take it face value and peace out.

Nor will I ever have another conversation in which my task is to prove I am a decent person, acceptable to the angels, in spite of having voted a certain way. I have had too many of these and even one is unacceptable. I will no longer be considering anybody who imposes such a degrading conversation, whether explicitly or by implication of their attitude, to be my friend.

I would never do that to somebody over politics and I will no longer be accepting it in my life, for any reason.

I am fine with disagreement, even heated disagreement, but the moment I get the sense that my basic moral status is on the table in your mind, we are not friends, the conversation is over, I no longer want to have any business with you ever again. Fin.

text:
Some thoughts on LLMs, language, and consciousness

At some point in our lives (hopefully pretty early) we learn language. How does this work? Absent formal training I think there are some pretty basic things we can establish:

1. We do not learn language entirely from the dictionary. This would be obviously circular.
2. We do not—entirely!—learn it by repeating the sounds other people make. Oh this is definitely part of the process, a huge part! In fact this is how we become competent with syntax and grammar before we even know what that means or ever set foot in a classroom.

In fact, so much important stuff happens in #2 that it's tempting to see it as the whole story. But try this thought experiment. Suppose you are hungry and your body could use a little sugar, and you are a bit thirsty. And then, as part of some offhand remark, I mention the word "apple".  Suddenly you're imagining an apple, its shape and color, what its juicy insides look like after you bite into it, its flavor and the texture and the sweetness and the juice.

I contend that THAT is what the word "apple" actually MEANS to you.

And I contend that this is a critical aspect of human language aquisition that is missing from point 2 above. We do not merely babble stuff that we happen to have heard other people before us babble. Words actually MEAN something to our EXPERIENCE, and this is actually what communication is all about. I don't know the jargon of linguistic theorists but in my head I use the word "anchor" for this. The word "apple" is ANCHORED in the experience an actual human has with an actual apple, and this is what gives the word meaning and this is what is transferred (however imperfectly, and communication is never perfect) to somebody else for whom the sound "eah-pull" is anchored in a similar enough experience.

Without anchoring, there is no "meaningful communication" (to introduce another piece of personal jargon). Meaningful communication happens between souls via the mechanism of anchoring.

When you read in a dictionary the definition of an unfamiliar word, there is an imaginative aspect to this. The dictionary definition of "citron" is "a shrubby Asian tree that bears large fruits similar to lemons, but with flesh that is less acid and peels that are thicker and more fragrant". In this definition are lots of things you'll already have anchors for: fruit, lemon, flesh (with context "of a fruit"!), acid, thick, fragrant, and more. You use these to establish an IMAGINATIVE sense of what a citron fruit is. In other words, you build an anchor. Not a great anchor, since you've never tasted an actual citron, but maybe enough of an anchor that if you come across an actual citron you might be able to say "hmm I wonder if this is a citron" and perhaps improve your anchor (if your suspicion is confirmed).

To meaningfully communicate with somebody, we are attempting to transmit an EXPERIENCE to them, to make them perform a similar imaginative exercise as in the example of reading a dictionary definition. The closer a reproduction of our own experience we are able to create in the imagination of the other person, the better we have communicated. This can be affected by many things, including but not limited to our own communication skills, their listening skills, and the similarity of their existing anchors to ours (in other words "can they relate").

This is what I mean by "meaningful communication" and this is what I mean when I make a statement most smart people disagree with like "An LLM cannot meaningfully communicate about qualia (or about ANYTHING!)".

Now there's an obvious caveat here: an LLM can "meaningfully communicate" in the same way a dictionary "meaningfully communicated" the definition of a citron. After all, you got the new anchors, right? So whose experience of a citron was communicated to you in the construction of that anchor? The dictionary's? Did the book have an experience and then tell you about it? Of course not; the author of the definition did.

LLMs are a combination of two things. First, the repetition of babble discussed in Point 2 above, except trained to an absurd perfection on everything anybody has ever written ever that's survived, which causes it to respond with a linguistic dexterity that lands on us as sublime, superhuman even. Secondly, all those words it's trained on contain the MEANINGFUL COMMUNICATIONS of millions upon millions of authors, all contributing to its superhuman babble-generating machinery, causing it to do for you what a dictionary can do for you, give you new anchors, only light years better than any dictionary.

So on the one hand, LLMs are exactly what they pretend to be: a near-miraculous tool for giving us missing pieces in our projects, in our personal lives, in our relationships, in our trauma recovery, in literally whatever we ask of it. We need a meaningful answer and it gives us one.

But on the other hand, LLMs deceive some of us into thinking they actually have THEIR OWN anchors.

They don't.

text:
I think people will see this and get lost in the weeds of "truth" and start talking about science or w/e (modern science's debt to schizo Christians like Newton is a related topic tho).

Like, all the stuff said here is true but specifically what is the "truth" everybody is running away from? It's not the theory of gravity. Nor is it even merely the acceptance of certain specific metaphysical propositions that an apologist is angling for, because then ESR's reply becomes valid: ultimately if we care about propositional truth why would we do anything except sciencemaxx?

But let me ask again, what truths do we RUN AWAY FROM?

And let me add: apart from stuff that would undermine a load-bearing worldview. That's obviously a very real and common cause of cowardice in the face of truth but I am looking at something deeper, something that even saying "ha ha yes of course my ego gets in the way of me accepting some things, I'm only human after all" can actually serve to COVER UP because you'd rather acknowledge such an ordinary failing than go deeper.

To me, obviously, it's our dark secrets (all you innocent doves who have no idea what I'm talking about can stop reading here). Our impure motivations, and our speech and actions we have taken from such, and the effect they have had on others. The harm we have caused, the path of damage we have left in our wake, and all the lies we've told ourselves and others to cover for it because even if we try on "moral nihilism" or other intellectualizing escape attempts, on some deep level we know this was bad and we do not feel excused and the thought of it all coming to light terrifies us.

Seen from this angle, the whole stuff about being willing to acknowledge truth dovetails perfectly with the other stuff about forgiveness of sins. In fact they're the same thing. This was never about science at all. Hearing "truth" and thinking science is a red herring. That's just not what the discussion is even about.

When I first started opening up to people about spirituality I put it in terms of "putting truth above all else" but for the sake of eliminating confusion I eventually started talking about "self-honesty above all else", which to my mind is the same and I personally prefer the word "truth" here but it seems to make me misunderstood when trying to communicate it to others.

text:
Morning and still thinking about this.

It's impossibility, I think. It's an internal signal saying "This way is blocked."

And not the sort of impossibility where it's noble to try anyway. It's the psyche itself shutting the door. Where time spent trying is genuinely wasted.
